questions.question: Every standard in the conductivity kit is marked in %IACS. The 100% point of that scale is defined as:
options: the conductivity of pure silver, the most conductive metal in common use
the conductivity of annealed pure copper at 20 °C — 58.0 MS/m
the highest conductivity the instrument can display at 60 kHz
the average conductivity of the aerospace aluminum alloys
questions.explanation: By definition, annealed pure copper at 20 °C — 58.0 MS/m, a resistivity of 1.7241 µΩ·cm — is 100% IACS. Every other metal is graded as a percentage of that copper reference: Ti-6Al-4V near 1%, the common aerospace aluminum alloys roughly 28–47%, commercially pure aluminum near 60%.

questions.question: Dedicated aluminum conductivity meters operate near 60 kHz — the same frequency used in the Z-curve lab. That choice works because 60 kHz:
options: places the aerospace aluminum alloys in the well-spread knee region, where a few %IACS produce a generous swing of the dot
is the only frequency at which aluminum supports eddy currents
drives the aluminum dots onto the air point, making balancing easier
maximizes the depth of penetration so the entire part thickness is measured
questions.explanation: Spacing along the Z-curve is not uniform: near the top a few %IACS barely nudge the dot, while around the knee — the bend of maximum normalized resistance — the same difference produces a generous swing. At 60 kHz the aluminum alloys (roughly 28–47% IACS) land in that best-separated stretch by design, giving sorting and heat-treat checks their sensitivity.

questions.question: During a heat-treat verification, a Level I standardizes the conductivity meter and measures 36.5% IACS on a 7075 part. The Level I's correct next step is to:
options: reject the part, since 7075-T6 should read near 32% IACS
accept the part, since the value falls between the T6 and T73 readings
record the reading and refer the accept/reject decision to a Level II or III working to the governing procedure
re-balance the instrument and re-measure until the part reads 32% IACS
questions.explanation: A Level I generates the data — standardize the instrument, measure, record. Deciding whether a conductivity value means acceptable or rejectable is evaluation, and that signature belongs to a Level II or III applying the governing procedure (aerospace heat-treat verification is formalized in MIL-STD-1537).

questions.question: Unlike the five nonmagnetic lab samples, a ferromagnetic part's operating point sits above the air point. When the probe is lifted from such a part, the dot:
options: stays put, because permeability locks the coil's impedance
moves farther above the air point as the coupling weakens
traces the same upward path as the nonmagnetic samples
moves down toward the air point — every lift-off curve ends there, whatever the sample
questions.explanation: Permeability raises the coil's reactance, so a ferromagnetic operating point sits above the air balance point. Lifting away still destroys the coupling, so the dot must return to the same air point — approaching it from above, moving down — while the nonmagnetic samples all approach it from below.

questions.question: A job calls for measuring the thickness of a nonconductive anodic coating on an aluminum part with an eddy current instrument. In this application, lift-off is:
options: a noise source that must be filtered out before the thickness can be read
the measurement itself — the dot's position along the lift-off curve reads the coating thickness, calibrated with shims or foils of known thickness on the bare substrate
cancelled out, because the coating conducts the eddy currents into the substrate
significant only when the coating is thicker than the standard depth of penetration
questions.explanation: The same physics that makes lift-off the dominant noise in crack scanning becomes the signal in coating work: a nonconductive layer holds the coil off the metal by exactly its own thickness, so the position along the lift-off curve reads the coating. Calibration uses shims or foils of known thickness on bare substrate — the measurement ASTM B244 governs.

questions.question: With rotation set so a 0.1 mm shim slide runs perfectly flat on the 7075 sample, you lift the probe completely off to air. The trace:
options: leaves the null horizontally, then bends away — only the near-surface working portion of the curved lift-off path can be made flat
runs perfectly horizontal all the way to the air point
runs vertically, because a full lift is a different variable from shim lift-off
vanishes, because the instrument cannot display an excursion that large
questions.explanation: The full lift-off curve is curved; only its nearly straight near-surface portion can be laid flat with a single rotation setting. That is why rotation is set on the first 0.1–0.2 mm — the range hand wobble actually occupies — and why a complete lift to air departs horizontally before bending away toward the air point.

questions.question: A surface crack and probe lift-off both weaken the eddy currents' opposing field, yet the two signals can be separated on the impedance plane. The reason is that:
options: a crack produces a far larger signal, and amplitude alone separates the variables
lift-off changes only the coil's resistance, while a crack changes only its reactance
a crack changes the distribution of current within the metal, while lift-off changes the coupling between coil and part — different causes move the operating point in different directions
the two cannot actually be separated; inspectors rely on scanning speed to tell them apart
questions.explanation: A crack breaks the closed eddy current loops, forcing current to crowd around its ends and dive beneath it; lift-off instead weakens how strongly the coil couples to the part. Because the causes differ, the dot moves in different directions — with lift-off rotated horizontal, the crack signal rises at a clearly separated phase angle. Amplitude is no separator: a few hundredths of a millimetre of hand wobble can out-swing a real crack.

questions.question: At 200 kHz, a flat-bottom hole 1 mm below the surface of the 7075-T6 block barely registers. The instructor recovers the signal by dropping to 10 kHz because the lower frequency:
options: raises the eddy current density at the part surface
increases the coil's inductive reactance, amplifying every signal on screen
eliminates the phase lag between surface and subsurface currents
stretches the standard depth of penetration to about 1.2 mm in that alloy, so usable current density reaches the hole — its signal reappearing rotated by the added phase lag of depth
questions.explanation: Standard depth of penetration, δ = 1/√(πfμσ), grows as frequency falls. At 200 kHz the hole sits beyond 3δ (about 0.8 mm), where only about 5% of the current density remains; at 10 kHz δ stretches to roughly 1.2 mm and the hole reappears. Deeper currents also lag by roughly 57° — one radian — per standard depth, so the subsurface signal shows up rotated relative to a surface notch.

questions.question: Every demonstration in this module begins with the instruction to null and measure well away from sample edges. An approaching edge disturbs the test because:
options: the coil's field extends beyond its windings, so the edge disturbs the eddy currents before the probe arrives and reads like a massive defect
edges are always work-hardened, which raises the local conductivity
the probe loses all lift-off response near an edge
edges reflect the magnetic field back into the coil, doubling its reactance
questions.explanation: The magnetic field wraps beyond the physical coil, so the edge effect begins before the probe reaches the edge — bending conductivity readings, distorting Z-curves and lift-off curves, and producing a response that can mimic a huge discontinuity. That is why every lab keeps the probe at least a probe diameter from edges and nulls on sound metal.

questions.question: With lift-off rotated horizontal during the combined demonstration, three more signatures appear. Which set of pairings matches the lab observations?
options: Probe rock — curls toward the air point; wall thinning — runs along the horizontal; steel touch — moves down the conductivity locus
Probe rock — runs along the horizontal; wall thinning inside about 3δ — curls the point toward the air point; steel touch — large deflection above the air point
Probe rock, wall thinning, and a steel touch all move the dot in the same direction; only amplitude distinguishes them
Probe rock — deflects above the air point; wall thinning — rises at a separated phase angle; steel touch — runs along the horizontal
questions.explanation: Direction identifies the variable; amplitude scores its size. Rotated flat, probe handling stays in the horizontal channel; thinning shows nothing until the wall drops inside the effective depth of penetration, then curls the operating point toward the air point as metal runs out — the basis of thickness measurement; and a ferromagnetic touch leaps above the air point because permeability raises inductive reactance. A surface crack adds the fourth signature, rising at its own separated angle.
